---
title: 'FW: When Tragedy Strikes, a Widower''s Perspective'
date: '2020-04-04T06:16:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: '(My Journal) Fed By Faith'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '6981322063922755515'
blogger_filename: '/2020/04/fw-when-tragedy-strikes-widowers.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/(My Journal) Fed By Faith/feed.atom'
---

<div class="WordSection1">  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>A males perspective on becoming a young widower, at 37 years old<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">What happens when our world shatters? In this article, we explain the heart-ache, challenges, and lesser-known quirks of a young widower's experience. We hope this provides insight into the life of loss that we lead. If you are a fellow   Widow(er), we hope you find comfort that you are not alone. If you are a friend/family of a Widow(er), we hope you find insight to help you be more supportive.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">**Image, lonely man***<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>Widow(er) A New Label<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Widower… Now that is a label I never anticipated wearing. <o:p>  </o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I knew husband, father, son, brother. Having been married once before I knew what it was to wear Divorced as a label. When things got really bad in my second marriage, I saw Divorced Single Father in my future. Having spent a lifetime battling   depression, I anticipated my wife may have to wear the term Widow someday (but I never really knew what that would mean for her, I just thought it meant she’d be free from my constant mood swings and fighting).<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">In my mind, when the Bible talks about taking care of “Widows and Orphans” the term “Widow” was referring to old women who lost their husband. It never occurred to me that a Widow might not have grey hair. It wasn’t a well thought out opinion,   rather, an underlying assumption of the term. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Then I became a 37-year old Widower, a month before my 38<sup>th</sup> birthday.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>My world shattered… devastated.<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">My life was suddenly and drastically altered forever. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">After 14.5 years of marriage, we finally started making it work. We had still been struggling but growing closer as a couple the past year and a half. We even renewed our romance a little and she was seven weeks pregnant. We sat on the   couch holding hands, her leg was up because it was hurting but they couldn’t find a clot and assured her it was a muscle strain, “keep it elevated and iced”. We talked about future plans, and giving each other the grace to pursue passions instead of money   in our creative endeavors. She said she wished we could make love because she really wanted me but her leg was hurting too much. She asked me to get some sleep as I had an early morning planned. I went to bed around 10:45 pm.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">At 1:45am, I woke up “Darrell… Darrell, get up! My leg stopped hurting and now I’m dizzy, you need to take me to the hospital, now!”<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Those were her last words. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I got up and grabbed her from behind, walked her 334lbs frame through the living room, she collapsed on the couch face-down. Then snorted several times in big heaving breaths. She got up twice, eyes rolled back in her head, stood wobbling,   and crashed face down on the cough again, knees on the floor. I counted breathes for 911 and she took her last one as the paramedics entered the room. Chest compressions for maybe 20 minutes led to a trip to the E.R. and chest compressions for another 45 minutes   or so… maybe longer, I can’t recall. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I still remember the doctor asking my permission to stop “interventions”. He said “Normally we get them back once or twice, but she hasn’t responded at all”. I said “Yes.”<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">What happened next was a whirlwind. My wife (baby inside) lay there on the table. Eventually I told the boys their mom was gone and they thought it was a joke at first. There were tears and yelling. Her mom and father showed up, they started   wailing. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Eventually we were at home, numb, staring at the wall. We ended up turning on the television show the boys had been watching with her, D.C.’s The Flash. We watched through every episode at least twice over the next few weeks.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I performed the Memorial Service. Her mom kept the ashes. <o:p>  </o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>The Finances Changed<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I stayed strong for everyone, but I was a mess. I built the excel sheets my late-wife was using to track bills, so it only took me two hours (thanks to Google Chrome saved passwords) to use her profile to sign in to each site, change contact   information, and get a handle on where we were financially.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">It was tight for the first few months, a GoFundMe page was set up and people poured in with gifts, which helped us stay afloat. Work donated money too.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Eventually, Social Security Survivor Benefits kicked in, which is the only reason we were able to keep living in the rental house we had. At approximately $300 per child, two children, it was the difference between eating and not eating.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>Our environment matched our hearts, in total disarray<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">The laundry stayed in a pile on the floor, clean or dirty. <o:p>  </o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Dishes piled in the sink so we moved to Paper Plates, Paper Bowls, and Plastic Utensils. I’d rather take a bi-weekly dump run than do any dishes.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Not that it mattered, because we didn’t make any meals for ourselves anymore. It’s not that we three boys didn’t know how. My late wife actually couldn’t cook, I was the chef of the home our whole marriage. I just couldn’t bring myself   to try. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">So we ate fast-food. Every day. For almost a year. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>My career trajectory was forever changed<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">My work friends and bosses gathered around me in support for the first few weeks leading up to returning to work, showered me with attention and even money.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Eventually I went back to work, but it was too early (start of week 4). I slowly realized, something was permanently changed inside of me. I couldn’t face people anymore. They would sit across the desk from me and I feared the next encounter…   each time. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">After a while, though, work noticed my clothes were never ironed and face scraggly. I couldn’t perform the basic functions of my job (in a bank, meeting with clients as a loan officer) and they didn’t have a better suited position to move   me to in the area. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I couldn’t do people face-to-face anymore. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">So I quit. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I found a call center job where I could be out of sight, but it still wasn’t far enough away because there were still people.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I often cried as I answered the phone. “This call may be recorded or monitored for quality assurance purposes, my name is Darrell with XYZ, how can I help today?” All the while, my voice cracking or straining from tears.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I think I may have found a home as a delivery driver, for now. I don’t know… figuring this one out.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>Catharsis: Letting go one item at a time<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">We started, slowly, cleaning through the house. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Early on, her mom wanted her clothes to eventually make a blanket for the boys (and be able to smell them). She then wanted all of her art-supplies for her workshop at the ranch. Every so often, another box of things was taken away, a few   at a time. It was cathartic, releasing a little bit at a time.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Eventually the boys wanted their own room, and the younger (11) wanted to be near mommy’s art room. So we cleaned out her art room, and converted it into his room. But left her desk and a few items in place for him to have a piece of her   around. <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">The art room was always the largest part of our moves over the years. A full quarter of all the items in our move went to her art-room. Once it was empty, the rest of the house was easier to go through.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">At this point, we’ve mostly stored anything we decided to keep with us. Her mom has the rest of the art supplies in her shop.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">The final item was the last big thing I bought her. A Wacom Intuos Pro Paper Edition, a tablet for artists to use to draw on the computer. Nobody in our home would be using it, her mom is no computer user really, so I took it down to the   pawn shop and sold it for a microphone to use for recording. My boys and I have talked non-stop for months about creating our own YouTube channel, so I decided to trade-in something we weren’t using for something we would.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Although I know it’s what she would want for us… it was hard letting it go. I prayed over it, and asked God to bring it to the right hands who would really do something amazing with it.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>The house changed us and we changed houses<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">We found we couldn’t function in the rental house any longer. I couldn’t walk passed the front door without looking at the love-seat and seeing her laying face-down, taking huge heaving breathes.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">She passed away on June 25, 2018. In Mid-March, 2019, we left our rental home and moved into a new house on a half-acre. The one thing I was adamant that I couldn’t bring with us were the couches. We have been in the house almost two months   and we are still sitting on Ikea Chairs and Lawn Chairs because I refused to bring those couches with us.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">And that was the right decision. It was cathartic letting those go. I always hated them anyway, and the purchase of those couches was the last big fight we ever had, one and a half years earlier.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">This was the first time I’d move without her. It was one, slow, painful box at a time. Every drawer held something she’d touched or laughed about. Her art room was the hardest and it was already done, so the rest went smoothly, if not quickly.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><b>The odd world of Relationships / Love / Sex<o:p></o:p></b></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">There’s a secret you won’t ever really be able to understand unless you are a widower. This does not apply to every widow/widower, but there are a large percentage (a majority even) in the groups I belong to that would identify strongly   with what I’m about to tell you.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Some, not all… &nbsp;Widow(er)s Get REALLY Horny.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">I’m not talking like “Hey, it’s been awhile…”; rather, I am saying that for many of us there is a marked increase in our libido that far exceeds anything we experienced while married or in our earlier single years. It’s as though the chemicals   in our brains are so far out of whack from grief, not sleeping, not eating, and the intense loneliness that accompanies loss… that the chemicals scream at you for happy hormones. It’s practically a manic obsession for many.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">As a Christian, I have a marked desire to follow God’s word regarding sex and marriage. However, none of that mattered early on. The pain was so intense, the desire to be held and loved was so intense, easing that pain is all that mattered.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Which is why, two months out from loss, when I met a girl who was selling a drum set on Facebook, I ended up dating her. She was, and is, amazing. She will make some lucky man a great wife someday. However, any competent pre-marital counselor   would have raised a hundred red flags at our paring. Communication styles, life-goals, child rearing… none of this was a good match.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">So when that ended approximately 45 days later… I was lonely again. Now it was worse because I’d lost my spouse and someone I thought would be my spouse in the same year. But the loneliness and ache was so painful that I needed the pain   to stop.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Now it’s 2018-2019, I haven’t been single since before my first marriage because I practically walked from one to the other without much or any dating between. So I had not been single since approximately 19 years old. Man… did things change   in those 19 years to 38 years old. There are apps, and online chats. I met two women through apps, and one through a local Facebook Singles group. One of them was short-lived. The other two remain friends to this day despite our knowing we wouldn’t work as   a couple. Mostly, with those two exceptions, the apps taught me that the women I liked, didn’t like me back.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Then a friend from my old town got my attention, she pushed at me and told me we’d be perfect for each other. I resisted, not wanting to fall to fast or early. Eventually, when she’d pushed hard enough, I fell. Then she decided I wasn’t   it and moved on without really ending things. Now I’d lost my wife and two women I thought would be a future wife in the span of 11 continuous months.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">So now I’m realizing that my friends and advisors were right. You should take time to be single, heal, and discover yourself before jumping into a new relationship. Maybe you take one or two years, as the common advise&nbsp; goes. One person   said one month for each year of marriage (for me, that would be 14.5 months). I really haven’t figured this one out yet, I just know that jumping in too early only increased the pain and complicated things.<o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><o:p>&nbsp;</o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal">Maybe I will pursue God as my Spouse. “Hey guys, this is my wife, Holy Spirit”.  <o:p></o:p></p>  <p class="MsoNormal"><br /></p></div>
